title: Cellebrite said it cut off Russia, but Russia used is tools anyway
slides: Cellebrite claimed it blocked Russia—or did it?
Russia accessed Cellebrite tools through intermediaries.
Sanctions didn't stop the software from being used.
Your own compliance tools may face similar leaks.
Audit your supply chain before the next breach.
thread_text:
1. Cellebrite's Russia problem

Their forensic tools were used in Russia despite the company claiming a ban.

2. Intermediaries bypass sanctions

Distributors and resellers kept the software flowing past official restrictions.

3. Sanctions were just theater

Government bans didn't cut off access—the tools were still in use.

4. Your own tools could leak

If Cellebrite's compliance failed, your audit systems may be compromised too.

5. Audit your supply chain today

Map every vendor's downstream access. Have you tested your weakest link?
video_script: But tools still reached Russia through partners.
Sanctions didn't stop the software flow.

Audit your supply chain before the next breach.

script: But tools still reached Russia through partners.
Sanctions didn't stop the software flow.
Your compliance tools could leak too.
Audit your supply chain before the next breach.

instagram.caption:
A Russian government agency had access to Cellebrite’s phone‑cracking software long after the company announced a ban.

The ban was real. The enforcement wasn't. Distributors and resellers kept the tools flowing — sanctions became theater.

If your content or publishing tools rely on third‑party platforms, your compliance chain could have the same backdoor. One unchecked reseller is all it takes.

Which vendor in your stack could be selling access to someone you banned tomorrow? Map that weak link today.
